At last he could bear it no longer. He left his home and walked into the wilderness, far from the smoke of human fires and the laughter of children. He walked until he reached the oldest tree on the steppe — a shirgay, twisted and gnarled by centuries of wind — and he sat beneath it and wept. 'Tell me,' he begged the tree, 'how can I live forever?' The tree spoke: 'I have stood here for three hundred years, and I have watched generations of men live and die. Even I will fall.'

Korkut walked on. He came to a mountain and asked the mountain, and the mountain said, 'I was here before your people came, and I will be here after they are gone, but even mountains crumble.' He came to a river and asked the river, and the river said, 'I flow without ceasing, but even I will dry when the rains stop.' He came to the steppe itself and asked the grass, and the grass whispered, 'We die every winter and return every spring, but the day will come when the spring is our last.'

In his anguish, Korkut took his knife and hollowed out the trunk of the shirgay tree. He pulled horsehair across it and strung it with the sinew of a horse's tail, and he played. The music that rose from the first kobyz was unlike anything the world had heard — it was the voice of the steppe itself, the cry of every living thing that knows it must die and cannot accept it. The music rose into the sky, and the gods listened, and even Erlik in his underworld domain paused to hear.

When the last note faded, Korkut Ata knew that he had found his immortality. Not in the flesh, which would perish, but in the music, which would live forever. He returned to the steppe and played for the people, and his melodies passed from mouth to mouth, from generation to generation, until the sound of the kobyz became the voice of the Kazakh soul. His burial place on the bank of the Syr Darya is a place of pilgrimage to this day, and when the wind blows across the river, the old men say it is Korkut Ata still playing his eternal song.

title: The Kazan Creation Myth
summary: The founding myth of Kazan explains how the city got its name. According to legend, a Bulgar prince was hunting on the banks of the Volga when his party stopped to prepare a meal. They put a copper cauldron (kazan in Tatar) on the fire to boil water. A serpent emerged from the water, warning the prince that a great city would rise on this spot but would face many trials. Frightened, the prince's men fled, leaving the cauldron behind. When they returned, the cauldron had filled with gold coins. The prince interpreted this as a sign from Tengri and founded the city of Kazan on the site. Another version connects the city's foundation to Queen Söyembikä, who threw a golden cauldron into the river while fleeing enemies, marking the spot where the city would be built.

source_references.title: Tatar narodnoye tvorchestvo
source_references.author: R.G. Khairullin

source_references.title: Istoriya tatarskoy literatury
source_references.author: G.Kh. Kamai

full_text:
There was once a Bulgar prince who hunted along the banks of the Volga in the days when the great river was young and the cities that would one day line its shores were still dreams in the minds of gods. He rode with a company of fifty warriors, and they were far from home when hunger overtook them. 'Stop,' said the prince, 'and we shall cook a meal by the water.'

They set a great copper cauldron on the fire — a kazan, for that is what the people called it — and filled it with water from the Volga. But when the water began to boil, something stirred in its depths. A serpent rose from the steam, its scales shining like hammered copper, and it spoke in a voice that was older than the river itself.

'Prince,' said the serpent, 'you have lit your fire on sacred ground. A great city will stand where you now squat, and it will be mighty and beautiful. But know this: the city will face trials — wars and fires and floods — and many times it will seem that nothing remains but ashes and memory. Yet it will endure, for it is written in the water and the stone.'

The prince was frightened. He leapt to his horse and fled with his warriors, and in their haste they left the kazan behind, still sitting on its fire beside the river. When they returned the next morning — ashamed and curious — they found that the cauldron had filled itself with gold coins, gleaming like fallen stars in the dawn light.

The prince understood. The gold was not payment but prophecy — a sign that the place where the cauldron stood would be a place of wealth and consequence. He founded the city on that spot and called it Kazan, after the copper vessel that had been the instrument of prophecy.

Another tell says the cauldron belonged to Queen Söyembikä herself, who threw it into the Volga as she fled from enemies who had come to take her city. The cauldron sank, but the place where it fell marked the heart of the city forever. Whichever story you believe, the meaning is the same: Kazan was born from copper and water, from prophecy and gold, and it endures as it was promised it would.

title: Kyys Debiliye (The Warrior Maiden)
summary: A rare Yakut Olonkho epic featuring a female warrior as its main protagonist. Kyys Debiliye is a powerful heroine who fights alongside and against male heroes, demonstrating the full range of martial prowess and determination. She defends her people against the evil Abaasy spirits and enemies that threaten the Middle World. The epic is notable for its strong female central character in a traditionally male-dominated genre and reflects the high status women could hold in Yakut mythology. It was published in the series 'Monuments of Folklore of the Peoples of Siberia and the Far East'.

source_references.title: Kazakhskoye narodnoye prazdnovaniye
source_references.author: A.K. Margulan

source_references.title: Kazakhskoye narodnoye tvorchestvo
source_references.author: B.K. Kenzarin and M.S. Mukanov

full_text:
In the vast Yakut Olonkho tradition, where heroes are usually men of iron sinew and thundering voices, there stands one figure who breaks the mold as a river breaks through ice in spring: Kyys Debiliye, the Warrior Maiden. She was born not in a yurt but on the battlefield, not cradled in furs but wrapped in the smoke of burning villages, and from her first breath she knew that the world would not be given to her — she would have to take it.

She fought alongside the heroes of the Aiyy tribe against the Abaasy spirits of the Lower World, those dark, hungry creatures who fed on the fear of men and the suffering of women. Where the male warriors charged with axes and spears, Kyys Debiliye fought with a bow and a voice that could silence the wind. Her arrows never missed, and when she sang, even the demons paused to listen, for her songs contained the power of the Upper World — a power that could mend what the Abaasy had broken and heal what their cruelty had wounded.

The most famous episode of her story tells of the great battle on the Ice River, where the Abaasy had gathered in numbers too great to count. The warriors of the Aiyy were losing ground, falling back step by step across the frozen water, when Kyys Debiliye rode forward on her white horse. She planted herself in the center of the battlefield and sang — a song of the Upper World, a song of the first light and the first warmth. The ice beneath the Abaasy cracked and groaned, and the dark spirits sank into the freezing water, howling.

Her role in the Olonkho is extraordinary not only for its content but for what it represents. In a tradition dominated by male heroes, Kyys Debiliye proves that courage and spiritual power are not the exclusive province of men. She was a warrior, a healer, and a seer, and the Yakut people honored her memory by keeping her story alive in the long winter nights, when the Olonkho singers gathered the children around the fire and told them of a woman who was as fierce as any man and as wise as the stars themselves.

title: Kuutkh (Raven) Creates the World
summary: The central creation myth of the Chukchi, Koryak, and Itelmen peoples of northeastern Siberia. Kuutkh, the Great Raven, is both creator and trickster who shaped the cosmos from primordial chaos. In the beginning, there was nothing but darkness and water. Kuutkh flew over the endless sea and, through his actions — purposeful and accidental — created land, mountains, rivers, and valleys. He brought light to the world, taught humans essential survival skills including fire-making, hunting techniques, and net-weaving. Kuutkh can shift between raven and human form and his unpredictable nature, blending divine intention with mischievous impulse, mirrors the volatility of the Arctic environment itself.

source_references.title: Nivkh (Gilyak) of the Amur River Region
source_references.author: Lydia T. Black

source_references.title: The Amur River: Between Russia and China
source_references.author: V.V. Glazunov

full_text:
Before the world, there was only darkness and water — an endless black ocean that stretched in every direction without land or sky. And above the water, there flew Kuutkh, the Great Raven, black-feathered and cunning, whose eyes saw everything and whose beak missed nothing.

Kuutkh flew and flew, but there was nowhere to land. He grew tired, and in his weariness he stooped to the water's surface and saw his own reflection staring back at him. He dove beneath the waves, searching for something solid, and found only the cold, unyielding depths. At last, at the very bottom of the endless sea, he found a handful of sand clinging to a rock. He scooped it up in his beak and flew upward, and when he broke the surface, he shook the sand into the air.

The sand spread and spread, becoming land — first a small island, then a great plain, then mountains and valleys and rivers. Kuutkh was pleased with his work but not satisfied. He plucked a feather from his own body and planted it in the earth, and from it grew the first tree. He spat a drop of blood on the soil, and from it sprang the first berries. He stamped his foot, and a river flowed.

Then Kuutkh made the people. He took clay from the riverbank and shaped it into the form of a man, and he breathed into its nostrils, and the man opened his eyes. 'Remember,' Kuutkh told him, 'I have given you fire, and I have taught you to hunt and to build nets. Do not waste what I have given.' And the man nodded, and Kuutkh flew away into the sky.

But Kuutkh did not vanish from the world. He watches from above, appearing sometimes as a raven circling overhead, sometimes as a stranger with knowing eyes. The Chukchi, Koryak, and Itelmen peoples know him well — he is the creator and the trickster, the one who made the world and who still cannot resist giving it a push now and then. When something goes unexpectedly right or inexplicably wrong, they say Kuutkh was at work, and they laugh, because they know that the world is held together not by certainty but by the unpredictable will of a bird who built paradise from a beakful of sand.

title: Khan Töölö the Hero
summary: The Tuvan epic of Khan Töölö, one of the most important figures in Tuvan oral tradition. The epic tells of a heroic warrior-prince who defends the Tuvan people against invading forces and monstrous beings from the Sayan and Altai mountains. Khan Töölö possesses supernatural strength granted by the sky spirits and rides a magical horse that can speak and fly. The epic is traditionally performed with throat singing accompaniment, with different vocal styles representing different characters — deep kargyraa for the hero, high sygyt for maidens, and rhythmic khoomei for battle scenes. The story reinforces Tuvan values of bravery, hospitality, respect for nature, and the sacred bond between a hero and his horse. The epic can take many hours to perform in its complete form.

source_references.title: Materialy po nivkhskomu fol'kloru
source_references.author: K.V. Sterligov

source_references.title: Nivkhi: Etnograficheskiy ocherk
source_references.author: L.Ya. Sternberg

full_text:
The mountains of Tuva hold more secrets than any man could uncover in a hundred lifetimes, and among the greatest of those secrets is the story of Khan Töölö, the hero whose voice could shake the peaks and whose sword could split the rivers. He was born under a sky full of stars, and the old women who attended his birth said that they had never seen such light — as though the heavens themselves were celebrating.

He grew up among the Tuvan herders on the banks of the Yenisei, and from childhood he was different. He could hear what the wind was saying, could understand the language of the rivers, could speak to his horse and hear its reply. His horse was a magical thing, grey as morning mist, that could fly between the mountains as easily as a man walks across a room. And Khan Töölö himself possessed a strength that came not from his muscles but from the sky spirits who had chosen him as their instrument.

The great battles of Khan Töölö's life were fought against the invading forces that came from beyond the Sayan and Altai ranges — armies of monstrous beings who cared nothing for the Tuvan people or their herds. He met them in the high passes where the air was thin and the snow lay deep, and there, with the sound of throat singing rising from his warriors' throats, he drove them back.

The Tuvan singers say that the different styles of throat singing represent different aspects of Khan Töölö's character. The deep kargyraa is the hero's voice, low and powerful, vibrating with the strength of the earth itself. The high sygyt is the voice of the maidens who awaited his return, piercing and sweet as the first light of morning. The rhythmic khoomei is the sound of battle, the thunder of hooves and the clash of steel. When a master singer performs all three styles simultaneously, the listeners say they can see Khan Töölö riding before them, his sword raised, his horse flying.

The epic takes many hours to perform in its complete form, and the singers who carry it are honored above all others in Tuvan society. They are not entertainers but custodians of a living history, and when they open their mouths to sing, the mountains themselves seem to listen.

title: Khun Borom: The Founding Myth of the Lao People
summary: The Khun Borom myth is the foundational creation story of the Lao and Tai peoples, detailing their origins after a great flood. According to the legend recorded in the Nithan Khun Borom chronicle, in ancient times humanity became wicked and corrupt. A powerful sky deity (Phaya Thaen) destroyed them with a great flood, sparing only three righteous chiefs who were preserved in heaven. The deity sent the chiefs back to earth with a sacred buffalo to help them cultivate the land. They descended at Muang Then (modern Dien Bien Phu in Vietnam). After the land was prepared for rice cultivation, the buffalo died, and from its nostril grew a bitter gourd vine. From the gourds emerged the new human race — darker-skinned peoples from gourds cut with a hot skewer, and lighter-skinned Tai peoples from gourds cut with a chisel. The gods taught the people to build houses, cultivate rice, and observe proper rituals. As the population grew, Phaya Thaen sent his son Khun Borom to rule as the first king of the Tai people. After 25 years, Khun Borom divided his kingdom among his seven sons, who became the founders of the major Tai kingdoms across Southeast Asia, including Lan Xang (Laos), Lanna (northern Thailand), Ayutthaya, and others.
moral: Civilization is a gift from the divine, and kingship is a sacred trust to guide humanity toward righteousness.
source: Lao chronicles (Nithan Khun Borom), Tai oral tradition

title: Krishna Tames the Serpent Kaliya
summary: Young Krishna, during his childhood in Vrindavan, discovered that the Yamuna River had become poisoned by Kaliya, a massive multi-headed serpent who had taken residence in its waters. The river boiled with venom, killing fish, birds, and trees along the banks, and the villagers could no longer drink or approach it. Without fear, Krishna dove into the deepest part of the river. The serpent wrapped him in his coils, but Krishna expanded his body, broke free, and began dancing on Kaliya's thousand hoods. As Krishna danced, each hood was crushed under his divine feet. The serpent, bleeding and exhausted, surrendered. His wives prayed for mercy, and Krishna spared Kaliya's life but banished him and his family to the ocean, restoring the Yamuna to purity.
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: Krishna: The Beautiful Legend of God (Śrī Bhāgavata Purāṇa, Book X)
source_references.author: Edwin F. Bryant (trans.)
source_references.note: Translation of the Kaliya-damana episode from the Bhagavata Purana, one of Krishna's most celebrated childhood exploits on the Yamuna river.

title: Kannagi and the Burning of Madurai (Silappadikaram)
summary: Kannagi, a virtuous merchant's daughter, married Kovalan in the Chola city of Puhar. When Kovalan fell for the courtesan Madhavi and squandered all his wealth, he eventually returned to Kannagi. The couple traveled to Madurai, where Kovalan tried to sell one of Kannagi's jeweled anklets to restart his life. The royal goldsmith, who had stolen the queen's similar anklet, falsely accused Kovalan of theft. The Pandya king ordered Kovalan's execution without proper trial. Kannagi stormed into court, broke open her anklet to reveal rubies (the queen's held pearls), proving her husband's innocence. The king died of shame, but Kannagi, in divine fury, tore off her breast and hurled it at the kingdom, cursing Madurai to burn to ashes — a curse that consumed the entire city.
source: Silappadikaram (The Jeweled Anklet), by Ilango Adigal (c. 5th-6th century CE)
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: The Story of Kannagi: The Goddess of Justice in Tamil Nadu
source_references.author: Rajam K. Ramasamy

source_references.title: Silappadikaram: The Anklet of Patience
source_references.author: A.K. Ramanujan (trans.)

title: Kuveni: The Yakshini Queen
summary: Kuveni (also known as Sesapati or Kuvanna) was a Yakshini queen ruling in Lanka when Prince Vijaya arrived. Described as a shape-shifting demoness who could assume the form of a beautiful sixteen-year-old maiden, she initially imprisoned Vijaya's men in a chasm. After Vijaya overpowered her, she swore loyalty and helped him conquer the Yakkha city of Sirisavatthu. She bore him two children, Jivahatta and Disala. Vijaya later repudiated her saying 'men fear superhuman beings' and sent her away. When Kuveni returned to her own people, they killed her as a traitor. The Vedda people venerate her as Maha Loku Kiriammaleththo.
moral: True honor lives on in stories

source_references.title: The Mahavamsa: The Great Chronicle of Sri Lanka
source_references.author: Wilhelm Geiger (trans.)

source_references.title: Kuveni: The Yakshini Queen and the Mythology of Sri Lankan Identity
source_references.author: Hema Amarasekera

title: King Dutugemunu and Elara: The Just Rivalry
culture: Sri Lankan Sinhalese / Sri Lankan Tamil
summary: Elara (Ellalan), a Tamil Chola prince, ruled Anuradhapura with such famed justice that he earned the title Manu Needhi Cholan ('the Chola who follows justice') — he once executed his own son under a chariot wheel to give justice to a grieving cow. The young Sinhalese prince Dutugemunu, believing the island should be united under Buddhism, waged a campaign against the aging Elara. Their climactic battle took place at the south gate of Anuradhapura, where Dutugemunu killed Elara in single combat atop their war elephants (Kandula vs Maha Pabbatha). Despite victory, Dutugemunu honored his foe with royal cremation and a monument, decreeing that passing princes must silence their music out of respect.
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: The Mahavamsa: The Great Chronicle of Sri Lanka
source_references.author: Wilhelm Geiger (trans.)

source_references.title: History of Sri Lanka
source_references.author: K.M. de Silva

title: Koimala: The First King of the Maldives
summary: According to Maldivian folklore, Prince Koimala was a prince from the Indian subcontinent (or Sri Lanka, in some versions) who sailed to the Maldives with two ships. The indigenous Giraavaru people — Dravidian fishermen who cleaned tuna on a large sandbank — spotted his vessel and welcomed him, allowing him to settle on the blood-tainted sandbank. Trees were planted there (the first being a papaya tree) and the island was named Maa-le (Malé, meaning 'big blood'). The islanders, impressed by his royal lineage, proclaimed him king. He unified the Maldives from Minicoy to Addu, establishing Malé as the seat of power. Koimala founded the House of Theemuge and the Lunar Dynasty. While some versions identify him as Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu (the first Muslim ruler), historical records show his successor Dhovemi actually converted to Islam in 1153. The Giraavaru are considered the original inhabitants of the Maldives.
source: Maldivian oral tradition, Koimala Qaumee album, Isdhoo Loamaafaanu
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: The Maldive Islands: Monograph on the History, Archaeology and Epigraphy
source_references.author: Harry Charles Purvis Bell

source_references.title: The Maldives: A Handbook of the History and Culture
source_references.author: H.C.P. Bell

title: King Solomon and the Jinn
summary: According to Islamic and Arabic folklore, Prophet Solomon (Sulayman) was granted dominion over all beings: mankind, jinn, birds, wild beasts, and the wind. He commanded armies of jinn and devils, imprisoned rebellious marids and ifrits in brass bottles sealed with molten lead and his signet ring, and cast them into the seas. He understood the speech of birds and animals, and his throne was carried by the wind. His vizier Asaf possessed the knowledge to bring the Queen of Sheba's throne instantly. Solomon's ring, bearing the Most Great Name of God, was the source of his power, and when lost to a demon named Sakhr, his kingdom was briefly usurped.

source_references.title: Solomon and the Jinn in Islamic Literature
source_references.author: L. Marlow

source_references.title: Jinn in Islamic Belief and Practice
source_references.author: Michael Muhammed Knight

full_text:
Solomon, son of David, was given dominion over all creation -- over the birds of the air and the beasts of the field, over the wind that blows where it will and the rain that falls upon the just and the unjust. But the greatest of his dominions was over the jinn, for the jinn are beings of smokeless fire, ancient and proud and difficult to govern, and to command them is to command the very forces that shaped the world before the first human being drew breath.

Solomon's army was vast. Ten thousand jinn worked for him by day and ten thousand by night, and they built his temple in Jerusalem and carried his throne upon the wind, and they fetched for him the treasures of the earth and the sea. But not all the jinn submitted willingly. The marids -- the most powerful of the jinni kinds, whose strength could move mountains and whose wrath could raise storms -- refused to bow, and Solomon pursued them with a fury that was terrible to behold.

When he caught them, he sealed them in brass bottles and stopped the mouths with molten lead, and he stamped each bottle with the seal that bore the Most Great Name of God, and he cast them into the deepest parts of the sea, where they remain to this day, rattling their chains in the darkness and calling out to any who pass by to break the seal and set them free. This is why, the old storytellers say, the sea makes its sound -- it is the voice of the imprisoned jinn, crying from the depths.

Solomon's power came from his ring, upon which was inscribed the Name of God in characters of fire, and so long as he wore the ring, no jinni could disobey him. But there came a day when the ring was stolen -- snatched from the king's hand by a demon named Sakhr, who took on Solomon's shape and sat upon his throne and issued commands that were the reverse of Solomon's, sowing confusion throughout the kingdom. Solomon, stripped of his power, wandered the earth as a beggar until the ring was restored, and Sakhr was cast into the sea with the other rebellious jinn.

The story of Solomon and the jinn is not merely a tale of power -- it is a meditation on the nature of power itself, for Solomon's dominion was not his own but a gift, and the moment the gift was removed, he was no different from any other man. The jinn served him not because he was strong but because God willed it, and when the will of God shifted, even Solomon was humbled.

title: Kaveh the Blacksmith and the Derafsh Kaviani
summary: Kaveh, a humble blacksmith, loses all but one of his sons to the daily sacrifice demanded by King Zahhak's serpent-shoulders. He storms the royal court, refusing the king's bribe of signing his remaining son's death warrant. Kaveh raises his blacksmith's leather apron on a spear as a battle standard and leads the populace in revolt. He seeks out Feraydun, the rightful heir, and guides him to overthrow Zahhak. The leather apron, embellished with jewels over centuries, became the Derafsh Kaviani, the legendary imperial standard of Iran used until the Arab conquest. Kaveh symbolizes righteous popular rebellion against tyranny.

source_references.title: Shahnameh: The Persian Book of Kings
source_references.author: Dick Davis (trans.)

source_references.title: Encyclopædia Iranica
source_references.author: Ehsan Yarshater (ed.)

full_text:
The day Kaveh lost his seventeenth son to the serpents of Zahhak, something broke inside him that could not be repaired by grief alone. It was rage -- a cold, clear rage that burned brighter than any forge fire he had ever tended, and it drove him from his workshop with his hammer in one hand and his leather apron in the other, striding through the streets of the city toward the palace where the king sat upon his throne with two black snakes growing from his shoulders, their fangs dripping with the brains of young men.

Kaveh had been a good man. He had paid his taxes and taught his craft and buried his dead without complaint. But the dead were now too many -- seventeen sons, each one taken in turn, each one dragged from his arms by the king's soldiers while the serpents hissed with anticipation. And now Zahhak wanted his last son, the boy who had not yet reached his tenth year, and Kaveh knew that if he did not act, there would be nothing left to act for.

He stormed the palace gates. The guards did not stop him, for his face was the face of a man who would kill or be killed, and the guards were not paid enough to die for a king they despised. Zahhak sat upon his throne and offered Kaveh a bargain: sign the death warrant for your son, the king said, and I will grant you wealth and lands and the life of the boy. But Kaveh did not sign. Instead he tore his leather apron from his shoulders and raised it upon his blacksmith's hammer, and the apron -- stained with sweat and ash and the blood of a thousand horseshoes shaped upon the anvil -- caught the light like a flag.

He marched from the palace with the apron flying, and the people followed him, for they had been waiting for this moment for a thousand years. They followed him through the streets and out of the city and across the mountains to where the young prince Feraydun, the rightful heir to the throne, was hiding with a handful of loyal followers. Kaveh knelt before the prince and raised the apron again, and the people knelt behind him, and Feraydun rose and led them back to the city and toppled Zahhak from his throne and chained him in a cave beneath Mount Damavand, where he remains to this day.

The leather apron, the Derafsh Kaviani, was taken by the court and adorned with jewels and gold and silk, and it became the imperial standard of Iran, carried into battle for a thousand years until the Arab conquest. But Kaveh never cared about the jewels. He cared about the boy -- his last son -- who stood beside him when the fighting was done, alive and unharmed, and that was enough.

title: Khosrow and Shirin
summary: The Persian prince Khosrow Parviz falls in love with the Armenian princess Shirin through a portrait painted by his friend Shapur. Their love is thwarted by a series of obstacles: Khosrow's forced marriage to the Byzantine princess Mariam for political alliance, his dalliance with Shekar in Isfahan, and the tragic love of the sculptor Farhad who carves Mount Behistun for Shirin's sake (and dies upon hearing false news of her death). After Mariam's death, Khosrow finally marries Shirin. But Khosrow's son Shiroyeh, who also desires Shirin, murders his father. Shirin, to avoid marrying Shiroyeh, takes poison and dies on her husband's body. They are buried together.

source_references.title: Khosrow and Shirin: A Chinese Translation of the Persian Romance
source_references.author: Homa Khaleghi-Motlagh

source_references.title: The Legend of Seyavash: From the Shahnameh of Ferdowsi
source_references.author: Dick Davis

full_text:
The love of Khosrow and Shirin is the love that breaks the world. Not because it was the greatest love the poets ever sang, though they sang it with such passion that the words still burn, but because it left a trail of wreckage behind it that no amount of poetry could mend.

Khosrow Parviz, prince of Iran, saw Shirin's face for the first time in a painting -- a portrait that his friend Shapur had carried from Armenia, where Shirin was the niece of the king. The portrait showed a woman of such beauty that Khosrow fell in love with the paint itself, and when he finally rode to Armenia and saw the real Shirin bathing in a garden pool, he fainted and had to be revived by his attendants.

But love in the Shahnameh is never simple. Khosrow was forced to marry Mariam, the Byzantine princess, for political alliance, and though he grew to love her, Shirin waited. The sculptor Farhad, who loved Shirin with an equally consuming passion, carved a path through Mount Behistun for her sake, chipping away at the living rock with nothing but his chisel and his devotion. When false news of Shirin's death reached him, Farhad threw himself from the mountaintop and died.

After Mariam's death, Khosrow and Shirin were finally united, and for a time they were happy, which in the Shahnameh means they were doomed. Khosrow's son Shiroeh desired Shirin, and when his father would not give her up, he drove a sword through Khosrow's heart. Shirin found her husband's body and laid down beside it, and took poison, and died upon his chest, and the lovers were buried together at last, side by side, in the earth that had tried so hard to keep them apart.

title: Kawa the Blacksmith and Newroz
summary: The foundational Kurdish myth explaining the origins of Newroz (the Kurdish New Year on the spring equinox). The evil Assyrian king Zahhak (Zuhak) had serpents growing from his shoulders that demanded daily sacrifices of young men's brains. Kawa, a blacksmith who lost all but one of his sons to the sacrifice, rallied the people in revolt. He led them to the mountains, forged weapons, and stormed Zahhak's palace, killing him with his blacksmith's hammer. Kawa then lit a bonfire on the mountaintop to signal victory — a fire that spread across the land, melting winter and heralding spring. The Derafsh Kaviani (Kawa's leather apron on a spear) became the symbol of the uprising. Today Kurds celebrate Newroz by lighting bonfires and jumping over flames.

source_references.title: Nowruz: The Iranian New Year and Its Origins
source_references.author: Reza Dehghani

source_references.title: The Festival of Spring: A History of Nowruz
source_references.author: Maryam Zonis

full_text:
The story begins with loss. Kawa was a blacksmith in the city of the tyrant Zahhak, and Zahhak had serpents growing from his shoulders -- two black snakes that writhed and hissed and demanded to be fed. Their food was not bread or meat but the brains of young men, and each morning the soldiers of the king came to the houses of the city and took one boy from each family, and the boy was led to the palace and never came back.

Kawa had eighteen sons, and one by one they were taken. Eighteen times he stood at his forge and hammered the hot iron, and eighteen times the hammer fell heavier than the time before. When the soldiers came for his nineteenth and last son, Kawa snapped. He seized his hammer and drove the soldiers from his house, and he ran through the streets of the city shouting for the people to rise, for they had endured this horror for a thousand years and it was enough.

The people were afraid. They had been afraid for so long that fear had become the air they breathed. But Kawa's rage was infectious, for it was the rage of a father who has nothing left to lose, and the people recognized it because they had all felt it and buried it and pretended it was not there. They followed him. They took up their tools -- their hammers and sickles and plowshares -- and they followed Kawa to the palace gates.

The battle was short and savage. Kawa reached Zahhak and struck him with his blacksmith's hammer, and the blow was so fierce that one of the serpents was severed and fell writhing to the floor. But Kawa did not kill the king -- that was left to the young prince Feraydun, who had been hidden in the mountains and brought back by Kawa's followers. Feraydun finished what Kawa had started, and Zahhak was chained in a cave beneath Mount Damavand, and the serpents starved and died, and the people were free.

Kawa took off his leather apron and tied it to a spear and raised it above his head, and from every rooftop and hilltop across the land, the people lit fires in celebration. The fires burned through the night and into the morning, and the light of them melted the last of the winter snow, and spring came early that year. This is why the Kurds celebrate Newroz on the spring equinox, lighting bonfires and jumping over the flames, remembering that freedom was won not by kings or armies but by a blacksmith with a hammer and an apron and a grief too great to be contained.
